claim: Multi-turn tool use is critical for building general-purpose agents that can handle long-horizon tasks across diverse settings
actionable: Design agents to iteratively use tools over multiple turns rather than in single interactions; train with reinforcement learning that rewards final task outcomes
source_title: Open Questions in Agentic RL — Will Brown (Prime Intellect)

claim: As agents perform more tool calls (e.g., 100+), credit assignment becomes highly tricky; intermediate turn-level rewards are needed instead of just final outcome rewards
actionable: Implement turn-level reward systems that give credit for individual agent actions, not just final success; consider multi-step verification as you scale
source_title: Open Questions in Agentic RL — Will Brown (Prime Intellect)

claim: Offloading specialized tasks to sub-agent VLMs (vision-language models) as tool calls reduces main agent context bloat and enables longer reasoning chains
actionable: Create specialized sub-agents for image understanding, summarization, or domain-specific tasks; expose them as tools to the main agent
source_title: Open Questions in Agentic RL — Will Brown (Prime Intellect)

claim: Reward models themselves should be reasoning models that break tasks into sub-criteria and rubrics for more reliable evaluation across long-horizon tasks
actionable: Train reward models using inference-time scaling; have them decompose evaluation criteria into measurable rubrics before scoring agent outputs
source_title: Open Questions in Agentic RL — Will Brown (Prime Intellect)

claim: Async, off-policy training with stale data is practical and robust for scaling agent systems; models can tolerate lag between action and training
actionable: Implement async pipelines where agents collect data, reward models evaluate asynchronously, and training happens with slight lag; this avoids efficiency bubbles
source_title: Open Questions in Agentic RL — Will Brown (Prime Intellect)

claim: Model weight merging works well for specialized models trained on different domains (code, math, languages); weights are orthogonal enough to simply average
actionable: Train decentralized models on different tasks independently, then merge weights by averaging; validate merged model on mixed-domain benchmarks
source_title: Open Questions in Agentic RL — Will Brown (Prime Intellect)
